category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}{{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}{{jalink|Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971|Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{jalink|Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1（{{jalink|859|Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{jalink|Usa Jingū}} in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, and later {{jalink|relocated}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076}}, the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 17 ({{jalink|1410|Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103}}. In {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 13 ({{jalink|1516|Q6212}}), the {{jalink|Imagawa clan|Q1764434}} of {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{jalink|Ōi clan|Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{jalink|Daiei|Q1193564}} 2 ({{jalink|1522|Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{jalink|jōri system|Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{jalink|Ōdaiki}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{jalink|1543|Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiō|Q1193841}} 9 ({{jalink|1500|Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{jalink|stone bridge|Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{jalink|Tenbun|Q1192853|Tenbun}} 4 ({{jalink|1535|Q6453}}) by {{jalink|Takato stonemasons|Q20041103}}. Made of {{jalink|granite|Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{jalink|Komainu|Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{jalink|1548|Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{jalink|1565|Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{jalink|1568|Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{jalink|gyokugan eyes|Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (15).jpg|Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine haiden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (16).jpg|Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (5).jpg|Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (4).jpg|Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (17).jpg|Torii === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} * [http://kubohachiman.com/ Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいまたくほはちまんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:shrine dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:山梨県の旧県社]] [[Category:山梨県の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:山梨県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:神功皇后を祭神とした神社]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}{{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}{{jalink|Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971|Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{jalink|Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1（{{jalink|859|Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{jalink|Usa Jingū}} in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, and later {{jalink|relocated}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076}}, the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 17 ({{jalink|1410|Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103}}. In {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 13 ({{jalink|1516|Q6212}}), the {{jalink|Imagawa clan|Q1764434}} of {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{jalink|Ōi clan|Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{jalink|Daiei|Q1193564}} 2 ({{jalink|1522|Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{jalink|jōri system|Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{jalink|Ōdaiki}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{jalink|1543|Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiō|Q1193841}} 9 ({{jalink|1500|Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{jalink|stone bridge|Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{jalink|Tenbun|Q1192853|Tenbun}} 4 ({{jalink|1535|Q6453}}) by {{jalink|Takato stonemasons|Q20041103}}. Made of {{jalink|granite|Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{jalink|Komainu|Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{jalink|1548|Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{jalink|1565|Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{jalink|1568|Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{jalink|gyokugan eyes|Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (15).jpg|Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine haiden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (16).jpg|Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (5).jpg|Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (4).jpg|Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (17).jpg|Torii === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} * [http://kubohachiman.com/ Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいまたくほはちまんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:shrine dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:山梨県の旧県社]] [[Category:山梨県の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:山梨県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:神功皇后を祭神とした神社]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}{{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}{{jalink|Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971|Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{jalink|Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1（{{jalink|859|Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{jalink|Usa Jingū}} in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, and later {{jalink|relocated}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076}}, the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 17 ({{jalink|1410|Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103}}. In {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 13 ({{jalink|1516|Q6212}}), the {{jalink|Imagawa clan|Q1764434}} of {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{jalink|Ōi clan|Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{jalink|Daiei|Q1193564}} 2 ({{jalink|1522|Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{jalink|jōri system|Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{jalink|Ōdaiki}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{jalink|1543|Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiō|Q1193841}} 9 ({{jalink|1500|Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{jalink|stone bridge|Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{jalink|Tenbun|Q1192853|Tenbun}} 4 ({{jalink|1535|Q6453}}) by {{jalink|Takato stonemasons|Q20041103}}. Made of {{jalink|granite|Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{jalink|Komainu|Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{jalink|1548|Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{jalink|1565|Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{jalink|1568|Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{jalink|gyokugan eyes|Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (15).jpg|Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine haiden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (16).jpg|Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (5).jpg|Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (4).jpg|Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (17).jpg|Torii === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} * [http://kubohachiman.com/ Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいまたくほはちまんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:shrine dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:山梨県の旧県社]] [[Category:山梨県の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:山梨県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:神功皇后を祭神とした神社]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: used local AI agents to translate these pages. Contrary to the description it was actually Claude Code for a few, OpenClaw with Codex, and regular Codex. Ollama and DeepSeek were too bad to actually use. see categorization since some of them are very questionable, especially if there was a massive decrease in page size or the {{jawiki| template is minimal or absent on the page

category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}{{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}{{jalink|Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971|Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{jalink|Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1（{{jalink|859|Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{jalink|Usa Jingū}} in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, and later {{jalink|relocated}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076}}, the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 17 ({{jalink|1410|Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103}}. In {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 13 ({{jalink|1516|Q6212}}), the {{jalink|Imagawa clan|Q1764434}} of {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{jalink|Ōi clan|Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{jalink|Daiei|Q1193564}} 2 ({{jalink|1522|Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{jalink|jōri system|Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{jalink|Ōdaiki}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{jalink|1543|Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiō|Q1193841}} 9 ({{jalink|1500|Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{jalink|stone bridge|Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{jalink|Tenbun|Q1192853|Tenbun}} 4 ({{jalink|1535|Q6453}}) by {{jalink|Takato stonemasons|Q20041103}}. Made of {{jalink|granite|Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{jalink|Komainu|Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{jalink|1548|Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{jalink|1565|Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{jalink|1568|Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{jalink|gyokugan eyes|Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (15).jpg|Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine haiden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (16).jpg|Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (5).jpg|Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (4).jpg|Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (17).jpg|Torii === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} * [http://kubohachiman.com/ Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいまたくほはちまんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:shrine dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:山梨県の旧県社]] [[Category:山梨県の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:山梨県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:神功皇后を祭神とした神社]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

Ollama and DeepSeek were too bad to actually use. see categorization since some of them are very questionable, especially if there was a massive decrease in page size or the {{jawiki| template is minimal or absent on the page

category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}{{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}{{jalink|Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971|Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{jalink|Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1（{{jalink|859|Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{jalink|Usa Jingū}} in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, and later {{jalink|relocated}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076}}, the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 17 ({{jalink|1410|Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103}}. In {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 13 ({{jalink|1516|Q6212}}), the {{jalink|Imagawa clan|Q1764434}} of {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{jalink|Ōi clan|Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{jalink|Daiei|Q1193564}} 2 ({{jalink|1522|Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{jalink|jōri system|Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{jalink|Ōdaiki}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{jalink|1543|Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiō|Q1193841}} 9 ({{jalink|1500|Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{jalink|stone bridge|Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{jalink|Tenbun|Q1192853|Tenbun}} 4 ({{jalink|1535|Q6453}}) by {{jalink|Takato stonemasons|Q20041103}}. Made of {{jalink|granite|Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{jalink|Komainu|Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{jalink|1548|Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{jalink|1565|Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{jalink|1568|Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{jalink|gyokugan eyes|Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (15).jpg|Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine haiden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (16).jpg|Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (5).jpg|Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (4).jpg|Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (17).jpg|Torii === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} * [http://kubohachiman.com/ Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいまたくほはちまんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:shrine dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:山梨県の旧県社]] [[Category:山梨県の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:山梨県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:神功皇后を祭神とした神社]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}{{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}{{jalink|Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971|Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{jalink|Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1（{{jalink|859|Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{jalink|Usa Jingū}} in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, and later {{jalink|relocated}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076}}, the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 17 ({{jalink|1410|Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103}}. In {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 13 ({{jalink|1516|Q6212}}), the {{jalink|Imagawa clan|Q1764434}} of {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{jalink|Ōi clan|Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{jalink|Daiei|Q1193564}} 2 ({{jalink|1522|Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{jalink|jōri system|Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{jalink|Ōdaiki}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{jalink|1543|Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiō|Q1193841}} 9 ({{jalink|1500|Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{jalink|stone bridge|Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{jalink|Tenbun|Q1192853|Tenbun}} 4 ({{jalink|1535|Q6453}}) by {{jalink|Takato stonemasons|Q20041103}}. Made of {{jalink|granite|Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{jalink|Komainu|Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{jalink|1548|Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{jalink|1565|Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{jalink|1568|Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{jalink|gyokugan eyes|Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{jalink|Usa Jingū}} in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, and later {{jalink|relocated}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076}}, the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 17 ({{jalink|1410|Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103}}. In {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 13 ({{jalink|1516|Q6212}}), the {{jalink|Imagawa clan|Q1764434}} of {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{jalink|Ōi clan|Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{jalink|Daiei|Q1193564}} 2 ({{jalink|1522|Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{jalink|jōri system|Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{jalink|Ōdaiki}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{jalink|1543|Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiō|Q1193841}} 9 ({{jalink|1500|Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{jalink|stone bridge|Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{jalink|Tenbun|Q1192853|Tenbun}} 4 ({{jalink|1535|Q6453}}) by {{jalink|Takato stonemasons|Q20041103}}. Made of {{jalink|granite|Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{jalink|Komainu|Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{jalink|1548|Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{jalink|1565|Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{jalink|1568|Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{jalink|gyokugan eyes|Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}{{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}{{jalink|Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}

{{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{jalink|Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1（{{jalink|859|Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{jalink|Usa Jingū}} in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, and later {{jalink|relocated}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076}}, the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 17 ({{jalink|1410|Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103}}. In {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 13 ({{jalink|1516|Q6212}}), the {{jalink|Imagawa clan|Q1764434}} of {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{jalink|Ōi clan|Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{jalink|Daiei|Q1193564}} 2 ({{jalink|1522|Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{jalink|jōri system|Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{jalink|Ōdaiki}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{jalink|1543|Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiō|Q1193841}} 9 ({{jalink|1500|Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{jalink|stone bridge|Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{jalink|Tenbun|Q1192853|Tenbun}} 4 ({{jalink|1535|Q6453}}) by {{jalink|Takato stonemasons|Q20041103}}. Made of {{jalink|granite|Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{jalink|Komainu|Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{jalink|1548|Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{jalink|1565|Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{jalink|1568|Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{jalink|gyokugan eyes|Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}{{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}{{jalink|Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971|Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{jalink|Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1（{{jalink|859|Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{jalink|Usa Jingū}} in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, and later {{jalink|relocated}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076}}, the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 17 ({{jalink|1410|Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103}}. In {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 13 ({{jalink|1516|Q6212}}), the {{jalink|Imagawa clan|Q1764434}} of {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{jalink|Ōi clan|Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{jalink|Daiei|Q1193564}} 2 ({{jalink|1522|Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{jalink|jōri system|Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{jalink|Ōdaiki}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{jalink|1543|Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiō|Q1193841}} 9 ({{jalink|1500|Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{jalink|stone bridge|Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{jalink|Tenbun|Q1192853|Tenbun}} 4 ({{jalink|1535|Q6453}}) by {{jalink|Takato stonemasons|Q20041103}}. Made of {{jalink|granite|Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{jalink|Komainu|Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{jalink|1548|Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{jalink|1565|Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{jalink|1568|Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{jalink|gyokugan eyes|Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}{{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}{{jalink|Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971|Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{jalink|Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1（{{jalink|859|Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Yamanashi|Q840610}}, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{jalink|Usa Jingū}} in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, and later {{jalink|relocated}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076}}, the {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 17 ({{jalink|1410|Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103}}. In {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 13 ({{jalink|1516|Q6212}}), the {{jalink|Imagawa clan|Q1764434}} of {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{jalink|Ōi clan|Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{jalink|Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{jalink|Daiei|Q1193564}} 2 ({{jalink|1522|Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{jalink|jōri system|Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{jalink|Ōdaiki}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{jalink|1543|Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiō|Q1193841}} 9 ({{jalink|1500|Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{jalink|stone bridge|Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{jalink|Tenbun|Q1192853|Tenbun}} 4 ({{jalink|1535|Q6453}}) by {{jalink|Takato stonemasons|Q20041103}}. Made of {{jalink|granite|Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{jalink|Komainu|Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{jalink|1548|Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{jalink|1565|Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{jalink|1568|Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{jalink|gyokugan eyes|Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610}}{{ill|lt=Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|WD=Q28684421|lt=Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}

{{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{ill||lt=Ken-sha|UNKNOWN}} |創建 = {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1（{{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{ill||lt=Usa Jingū|UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751}}, and later {{ill||lt=relocated|UNKNOWN}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076}}, the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1410|Q6151|WD=Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103|WD=Q11546103}}. In {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1516|Q6212|WD=Q6212}}), the {{ill|lt=Imagawa clan|Q1764434|WD=Q1764434}} of {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{ill|lt=Ōi clan|Q11432196|WD=Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1519|Q6278|WD=Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628|WD=Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{ill|lt=Daiei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1522|Q6299|WD=Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{ill|lt=jōri system|Q11523645|WD=Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{ill||lt=Ōdaiki|UNKNOWN}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{ill|lt=1543|Q6523|WD=Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1500|Q6126|WD=Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{ill|lt=stone bridge|Q19839143|WD=Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1535|Q6453|WD=Q6453}}) by {{ill|lt=Takato stonemasons|Q20041103|WD=Q20041103}}. Made of {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|WD=Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{ill|lt=Komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{ill|lt=1548|Q6551|WD=Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{ill|lt=1565|Q6634|WD=Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1568|Q6648|WD=Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{ill|lt=gyokugan eyes|Q17990421|WD=Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610}}{{ill|lt=Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|WD=Q28684421|lt=Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-sha|UNKNOWN}} |創建 = {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1（{{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, an enshrinement from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usa Jingū|UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751}}, and later {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=relocated|UNKNOWN}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076}}, the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1410|Q6151|WD=Q6151}}) by the Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103|WD=Q11546103}}. In {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1516|Q6212|WD=Q6212}}), the {{ill|lt=Imagawa clan|Q1764434|WD=Q1764434}} of {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170}} joined with the local kunishū {{ill|lt=Ōi clan|Q11432196|WD=Q11432196}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1519|Q6278|WD=Q6278}}) by Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628|WD=Q1192628}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{ill|lt=Daiei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1522|Q6299|WD=Q6299}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{ill|lt=jōri system|Q11523645|WD=Q11523645}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōdaiki|UNKNOWN}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{ill|lt=1543|Q6523|WD=Q6523}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1500|Q6126|WD=Q6126}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{ill|lt=stone bridge|Q19839143|WD=Q19839143}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1535|Q6453|WD=Q6453}}) by {{ill|lt=Takato stonemasons|Q20041103|WD=Q20041103}}. Made of {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|WD=Q41177}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{ill|lt=Komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{ill|lt=1548|Q6551|WD=Q6551}}), Eiroku 8 ({{ill|lt=1565|Q6634|WD=Q6634}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1568|Q6648|WD=Q6648}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{ill|lt=gyokugan eyes|Q17990421|WD=Q17990421}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden.
