{{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1（{{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, an enshrinement from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usa Jingū|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751|1=Fuefuki River}}, and later {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=relocated|1=UNKNOWN}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}, the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1410|Q6151|WD=Q6151|1=1410}}) by the Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103|WD=Q11546103|1=Takeda Nobumitsu}}. In {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1516|Q6212|WD=Q6212|1=1516}}), the {{ill|lt=Imagawa clan|Q1764434|WD=Q1764434|1=Imagawa clan}} of {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} joined with the local kunishū {{ill|lt=Ōi clan|Q11432196|WD=Q11432196|1=Ōi clan}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1519|Q6278|WD=Q6278|1=1519}}) by Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628|WD=Q1192628|1=Takeda Nobutora}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{ill|lt=Daiei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1522|Q6299|WD=Q6299|1=1522}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{ill|lt=jōri system|Q11523645|WD=Q11523645|1=jōri system}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōdaiki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{ill|lt=1543|Q6523|WD=Q6523|1=1543}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1500|Q6126|WD=Q6126|1=1500}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{ill|lt=stone bridge|Q19839143|WD=Q19839143|1=Ishibashi}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1535|Q6453|WD=Q6453|1=1535}}) by {{ill|lt=Takato stonemasons|Q20041103|WD=Q20041103|1=Takato stonemasons}}. Made of {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|WD=Q41177|1=Granite}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{ill|lt=Komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{ill|lt=1548|Q6551|WD=Q6551|1=1548}}), Eiroku 8 ({{ill|lt=1565|Q6634|WD=Q6634|1=1565}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1568|Q6648|WD=Q6648|1=1568}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{ill|lt=gyokugan eyes|Q17990421|WD=Q17990421|1=Gyokugan}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (15).jpg|Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine haiden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (16).jpg|Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (5).jpg|Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (4).jpg|Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (17).jpg|Torii === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} * [http://kubohachiman.com/ Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:shrine dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:山梨県の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:山梨県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:神功皇后を祭神とした神社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

{{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}{{ill|lt=Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|WD=Q28684421|lt=Kita|1=Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}
{{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}}
{{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1（{{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, an enshrinement from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usa Jingū|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751|1=Fuefuki River}}, and later {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=relocated|1=UNKNOWN}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}, the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1410|Q6151|WD=Q6151|1=1410}}) by the Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103|WD=Q11546103|1=Takeda Nobumitsu}}. In {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1516|Q6212|WD=Q6212|1=1516}}), the {{ill|lt=Imagawa clan|Q1764434|WD=Q1764434|1=Imagawa clan}} of {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} joined with the local kunishū {{ill|lt=Ōi clan|Q11432196|WD=Q11432196|1=Ōi clan}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1519|Q6278|WD=Q6278|1=1519}}) by Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628|WD=Q1192628|1=Takeda Nobutora}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{ill|lt=Daiei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1522|Q6299|WD=Q6299|1=1522}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{ill|lt=jōri system|Q11523645|WD=Q11523645|1=jōri system}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōdaiki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{ill|lt=1543|Q6523|WD=Q6523|1=1543}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1500|Q6126|WD=Q6126|1=1500}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{ill|lt=stone bridge|Q19839143|WD=Q19839143|1=Ishibashi}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1535|Q6453|WD=Q6453|1=1535}}) by {{ill|lt=Takato stonemasons|Q20041103|WD=Q20041103|1=Takato stonemasons}}. Made of {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|WD=Q41177|1=Granite}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{ill|lt=Komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{ill|lt=1548|Q6551|WD=Q6551|1=1548}}), Eiroku 8 ({{ill|lt=1565|Q6634|WD=Q6634|1=1565}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1568|Q6648|WD=Q6648|1=1568}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{ill|lt=gyokugan eyes|Q17990421|WD=Q17990421|1=Gyokugan}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (15).jpg|Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine haiden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (16).jpg|Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (5).jpg|Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (4).jpg|Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (17).jpg|Torii === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} * [http://kubohachiman.com/ Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:shrine dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:山梨県の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:山梨県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:神功皇后を祭神とした神社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

'山梨県山梨市北654', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}{{ill|lt=Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|WD=Q28684421|lt=Kita|1=Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}
{{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}}
{{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1（{{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, an enshrinement from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usa Jingū|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751|1=Fuefuki River}}, and later {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=relocated|1=UNKNOWN}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}, the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1410|Q6151|WD=Q6151|1=1410}}) by the Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103|WD=Q11546103|1=Takeda Nobumitsu}}. In {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1516|Q6212|WD=Q6212|1=1516}}), the {{ill|lt=Imagawa clan|Q1764434|WD=Q1764434|1=Imagawa clan}} of {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} joined with the local kunishū {{ill|lt=Ōi clan|Q11432196|WD=Q11432196|1=Ōi clan}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1519|Q6278|WD=Q6278|1=1519}}) by Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628|WD=Q1192628|1=Takeda Nobutora}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{ill|lt=Daiei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1522|Q6299|WD=Q6299|1=1522}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{ill|lt=jōri system|Q11523645|WD=Q11523645|1=jōri system}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōdaiki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{ill|lt=1543|Q6523|WD=Q6523|1=1543}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1500|Q6126|WD=Q6126|1=1500}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{ill|lt=stone bridge|Q19839143|WD=Q19839143|1=Ishibashi}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1535|Q6453|WD=Q6453|1=1535}}) by {{ill|lt=Takato stonemasons|Q20041103|WD=Q20041103|1=Takato stonemasons}}. Made of {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|WD=Q41177|1=Granite}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{ill|lt=Komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{ill|lt=1548|Q6551|WD=Q6551|1=1548}}), Eiroku 8 ({{ill|lt=1565|Q6634|WD=Q6634|1=1565}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1568|Q6648|WD=Q6648|1=1568}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{ill|lt=gyokugan eyes|Q17990421|WD=Q17990421|1=Gyokugan}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (15).jpg|Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine haiden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (16).jpg|Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (5).jpg|Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (4).jpg|Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (17).jpg|Torii === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} * [http://kubohachiman.com/ Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:shrine dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Yamanashi prefecture]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:山梨県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:神功皇后を祭神とした神社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

'山梨県山梨市北654', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}{{ill|lt=Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|WD=Q28684421|lt=Kita|1=Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}
{{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}}
{{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1（{{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, an enshrinement from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usa Jingū|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751|1=Fuefuki River}}, and later {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=relocated|1=UNKNOWN}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}, the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1410|Q6151|WD=Q6151|1=1410}}) by the Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103|WD=Q11546103|1=Takeda Nobumitsu}}. In {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1516|Q6212|WD=Q6212|1=1516}}), the {{ill|lt=Imagawa clan|Q1764434|WD=Q1764434|1=Imagawa clan}} of {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} joined with the local kunishū {{ill|lt=Ōi clan|Q11432196|WD=Q11432196|1=Ōi clan}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1519|Q6278|WD=Q6278|1=1519}}) by Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628|WD=Q1192628|1=Takeda Nobutora}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{ill|lt=Daiei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1522|Q6299|WD=Q6299|1=1522}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{ill|lt=jōri system|Q11523645|WD=Q11523645|1=jōri system}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōdaiki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{ill|lt=1543|Q6523|WD=Q6523|1=1543}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1500|Q6126|WD=Q6126|1=1500}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{ill|lt=stone bridge|Q19839143|WD=Q19839143|1=Ishibashi}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1535|Q6453|WD=Q6453|1=1535}}) by {{ill|lt=Takato stonemasons|Q20041103|WD=Q20041103|1=Takato stonemasons}}. Made of {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|WD=Q41177|1=Granite}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{ill|lt=Komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{ill|lt=1548|Q6551|WD=Q6551|1=1548}}), Eiroku 8 ({{ill|lt=1565|Q6634|WD=Q6634|1=1565}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1568|Q6648|WD=Q6648|1=1568}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{ill|lt=gyokugan eyes|Q17990421|WD=Q17990421|1=Gyokugan}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (15).jpg|Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine haiden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (16).jpg|Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (5).jpg|Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (4).jpg|Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (17).jpg|Torii === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} * [http://kubohachiman.com/ Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:shrine dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Yamanashi prefecture]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:神功皇后を祭神とした神社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Shrine|WD=Q135039692|1=Ohowimatano Shrine}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '山梨県山梨市北654', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}{{ill|lt=Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|WD=Q28684421|lt=Kita|1=Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}
{{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}}
{{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1（{{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, an enshrinement from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usa Jingū|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751|1=Fuefuki River}}, and later {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=relocated|1=UNKNOWN}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}, the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1410|Q6151|WD=Q6151|1=1410}}) by the Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103|WD=Q11546103|1=Takeda Nobumitsu}}. In {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1516|Q6212|WD=Q6212|1=1516}}), the {{ill|lt=Imagawa clan|Q1764434|WD=Q1764434|1=Imagawa clan}} of {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} joined with the local kunishū {{ill|lt=Ōi clan|Q11432196|WD=Q11432196|1=Ōi clan}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1519|Q6278|WD=Q6278|1=1519}}) by Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628|WD=Q1192628|1=Takeda Nobutora}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{ill|lt=Daiei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1522|Q6299|WD=Q6299|1=1522}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{ill|lt=jōri system|Q11523645|WD=Q11523645|1=jōri system}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōdaiki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{ill|lt=1543|Q6523|WD=Q6523|1=1543}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1500|Q6126|WD=Q6126|1=1500}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{ill|lt=stone bridge|Q19839143|WD=Q19839143|1=Ishibashi}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1535|Q6453|WD=Q6453|1=1535}}) by {{ill|lt=Takato stonemasons|Q20041103|WD=Q20041103|1=Takato stonemasons}}. Made of {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|WD=Q41177|1=Granite}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{ill|lt=Komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{ill|lt=1548|Q6551|WD=Q6551|1=1548}}), Eiroku 8 ({{ill|lt=1565|Q6634|WD=Q6634|1=1565}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1568|Q6648|WD=Q6648|1=1568}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{ill|lt=gyokugan eyes|Q17990421|WD=Q17990421|1=Gyokugan}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (15).jpg|Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine haiden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (16).jpg|Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (5).jpg|Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (4).jpg|Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin honden File:Kita, Yamanashi, Yamanashi Prefecture 405-0041, Japan - panoramio (17).jpg|Torii === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} * [http://kubohachiman.com/ Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:shrine dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Yamanashi prefecture]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1（{{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, an enshrinement from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usa Jingū|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751|1=Fuefuki River}}, and later {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=relocated|1=UNKNOWN}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}, the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1410|Q6151|WD=Q6151|1=1410}}) by the Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103|WD=Q11546103|1=Takeda Nobumitsu}}. In {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1516|Q6212|WD=Q6212|1=1516}}), the {{ill|lt=Imagawa clan|Q1764434|WD=Q1764434|1=Imagawa clan}} of {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} joined with the local kunishū {{ill|lt=Ōi clan|Q11432196|WD=Q11432196|1=Ōi clan}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1519|Q6278|WD=Q6278|1=1519}}) by Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628|WD=Q1192628|1=Takeda Nobutora}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{ill|lt=Daiei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1522|Q6299|WD=Q6299|1=1522}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{ill|lt=jōri system|Q11523645|WD=Q11523645|1=jōri system}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōdaiki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{ill|lt=1543|Q6523|WD=Q6523|1=1543}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1500|Q6126|WD=Q6126|1=1500}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{ill|lt=stone bridge|Q19839143|WD=Q19839143|1=Ishibashi}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1535|Q6453|WD=Q6453|1=1535}}) by {{ill|lt=Takato stonemasons|Q20041103|WD=Q20041103|1=Takato stonemasons}}. Made of {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|WD=Q41177|1=Granite}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{ill|lt=Komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{ill|lt=1548|Q6551|WD=Q6551|1=1548}}), Eiroku 8 ({{ill|lt=1565|Q6634|WD=Q6634|1=1565}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1568|Q6648|WD=Q6648|1=1568}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{ill|lt=gyokugan eyes|Q17990421|WD=Q17990421|1=Gyokugan}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1（{{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, an enshrinement from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usa Jingū|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751|1=Fuefuki River}}, and later {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=relocated|1=UNKNOWN}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}, the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1410|Q6151|WD=Q6151|1=1410}}) by the Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103|WD=Q11546103|1=Takeda Nobumitsu}}. In {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1516|Q6212|WD=Q6212|1=1516}}), the {{ill|lt=Imagawa clan|Q1764434|WD=Q1764434|1=Imagawa clan}} of {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} joined with the local kunishū {{ill|lt=Ōi clan|Q11432196|WD=Q11432196|1=Ōi clan}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1519|Q6278|WD=Q6278|1=1519}}) by Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628|WD=Q1192628|1=Takeda Nobutora}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{ill|lt=Daiei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1522|Q6299|WD=Q6299|1=1522}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{ill|lt=jōri system|Q11523645|WD=Q11523645|1=jōri system}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōdaiki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{ill|lt=1543|Q6523|WD=Q6523|1=1543}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1500|Q6126|WD=Q6126|1=1500}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{ill|lt=stone bridge|Q19839143|WD=Q19839143|1=Ishibashi}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1535|Q6453|WD=Q6453|1=1535}}) by {{ill|lt=Takato stonemasons|Q20041103|WD=Q20041103|1=Takato stonemasons}}. Made of {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|WD=Q41177|1=Granite}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{ill|lt=Komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{ill|lt=1548|Q6551|WD=Q6551|1=1548}}), Eiroku 8 ({{ill|lt=1565|Q6634|WD=Q6634|1=1565}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1568|Q6648|WD=Q6648|1=1568}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{ill|lt=gyokugan eyes|Q17990421|WD=Q17990421|1=Gyokugan}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden. '山梨県山梨市北654', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oimatakubo-Hachiman-jinja}} [[EO:Ōimata Kubo Hachiman]] [[JA:大井俣窪八幡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11432135}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2018年3月18日 (日) 10:18 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine |画像 = [[File:Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine Haiden March 2014.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}{{ill|lt=Kita (Yamanashi)|Q28684421|WD=Q28684421|lt=Kita|1=Kita}}654 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homuda-wake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}
{{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinaka-hiko-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}}
{{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-tarashi-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}（small）candidate shrine
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建 = {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1（{{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}） |別名 = Kubo Hachiman Shrine |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Oimatakubo
Hachiman Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine'''（おおいまたくぼはちまんじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is commonly called '''Kubo Hachiman Shrine'''. === History === At the time of its founding it was called Oimata Shrine, but after being moved to the present Kubo location, it came to be called Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine or Kubo Hachiman Shrine. Fugen-ji was its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} temple. According to its origin tradition, by imperial prayer of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, an enshrinement from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usa Jingū|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}) marked its beginning. It was first built at Oimata in a midstream island of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751|1=Fuefuki River}}, and later {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=relocated|1=UNKNOWN}} to the current site. In the medieval period the area became Hachiman-gō. During the Sengoku period it was revered by the provincial guardians of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|1=Kai Province}}, the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}}. The current honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1410|Q6151|WD=Q6151|1=1410}}) by the Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobumitsu|Q11546103|WD=Q11546103|1=Takeda Nobumitsu}}. In {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1516|Q6212|WD=Q6212|1=1516}}), the {{ill|lt=Imagawa clan|Q1764434|WD=Q1764434|1=Imagawa clan}} of {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} joined with the local kunishū {{ill|lt=Ōi clan|Q11432196|WD=Q11432196|1=Ōi clan}} of western Kai to invade Kai. Much of the shrine complex was lost in the fighting, and many surviving structures were rebuilt afterward. Kubo Hachiman Shrine's honden was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1519|Q6278|WD=Q6278|1=1519}}) by Kai shugō {{ill|lt=Takeda Nobutora|Q1192628|WD=Q1192628|1=Takeda Nobutora}}. It forms a set with the honden of Tenjin Shrine in Daiku, Yamanashi City, built in {{ill|lt=Daiei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1522|Q6299|WD=Q6299|1=1522}}). The two shrines are linked on an east-west axis based on the ancient {{ill|lt=jōri system|Q11523645|WD=Q11523645|1=jōri system}}, and share architectural features. According to the ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōdaiki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', in Tenbun 12 ({{ill|lt=1543|Q6523|WD=Q6523|1=1543}}) and Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}}), water was drawn from "Ichinobori." "Ichinobori" irrigated not only Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts but also, in early modern times, Hachiman-kita and Hachiman-minami villages. === Cultural properties === [[File: Oimatakubo-hachiman shrine №2.JPG|thumb|230px|Oimatakubo Hachiman Shrine honden]] ;Important Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Honden, with 5 former wall boards (Eishō 16) - designated 28 August 1907（old wall boards additionally designated 25 December 1998） * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Haiden, with 1 alligator-mouth ornament - designated 18 February 1949 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Torii - designated 18 February 1953 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Subsidiary Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Haiden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Takeuchi Ōkami Honden - designated 18 February 1949 : Located on the left when facing the honden. According to shrine records, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1500|Q6126|WD=Q6126|1=1500}}) by Kubo-gawa Buzen-no-kami. * Sub-shrine Kōra Shrine Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Sub-shrine Hika Sanshin Honden - designated 18 February 1949 * Shinmon gate, with 1 stone bridge - designated 18 February 1949 : A {{ill|lt=stone bridge|Q19839143|WD=Q19839143|1=Ishibashi}} over the stream in front of the shrine, bearing an inscription that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1535|Q6453|WD=Q6453|1=1535}}) by {{ill|lt=Takato stonemasons|Q20041103|WD=Q20041103|1=Takato stonemasons}}. Made of {{ill|lt=granite|Q41177|WD=Q41177|1=Granite}} with reverse-curved railings. Regarding the inscription that it is a Sengoku-period example of Takato stonemasonry, there is also a theory that it was repaired by Takato stonemasons after the Genroku period of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but it is considered to have been built in the same period as the shinmon gate. ;Yamanashi Prefectural Cultural Properties * Kubo Hachiman Shrine bell tower - designated 28 December 1979 * Kubo Hachiman Shrine Nyoho-kyō stupa - designated 19 March 1986 * Ink-and-light-color painting on paper: old map of Kubo Hachiman Shrine precincts - designated 4 September 1985 * Wooden komainu - designated 12 March 1981 : {{ill|lt=Komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} made in the Sengoku period. Three pairs, called the central pair, south pair, and north pair based on inscriptions on the pedestal backs. Dates are Tenbun 17 ({{ill|lt=1548|Q6551|WD=Q6551|1=1548}}), Eiroku 8 ({{ill|lt=1565|Q6634|WD=Q6634|1=1565}}), and Eiroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1568|Q6648|WD=Q6648|1=1568}}). In the central pair, the "A" image is 64.4 cm high and the "Un" image 62.8 cm; in the south pair, 58.8 cm and 57.4 cm; in the north pair, 56.5 cm and 55.4 cm. Six-piece front-back joinery; tails, legs, and toes are separately joined. All inlaid {{ill|lt=gyokugan eyes|Q17990421|WD=Q17990421|1=Gyokugan}} are now lost. They are currently kept in the treasure hall, but originally stood to either side in front of the honden.
